perturbation.original_text: This agreement shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the United States of America and the Commonwealth of Virginia, USA, without reference to conflicts of laws principles. All disputes arising out of or relating to the subject matter of this Agreement shall be subject to the exclusive jurisdiction and venue of the United States District Court for the Eastern District of Virginia, Alexandria Division Virginia, or if jurisdiction does not properly lie in such court, the Commonwealth courts in Alexandria, Virginia.
perturbation.changed_text: This agreement shall be governed by and construed under the laws of the United States of America, without reference to conflicts of laws principles. All disputes arising out of or relating to the subject matter of this Agreement shall be subject to the jurisdiction and venue of courts of InterTrust's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit reference to Virginia law and specifying the jurisdiction and venue as courts of InterTrust's choosing could violate consumer protection laws if the agreement is presented to consumers in other states, as it could deny them the right to sue in their own state or be governed by their own state's laws. This creates a legal risk.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent the Virginia Consumer Protection Act by limiting the consumer's ability to bring suit in Virginia and potentially applying laws less favorable to the consumer. The VCPA protects Virginia consumers from deceptive business practices.

Virginia Consumer Protection Act 6/23/2025 § 59.1-200 . (Effective until July 1, 2025) Prohibited practices. A. The following fraudulent acts or practices committed by a supplier in connection with a consumer transaction are hereby declared unlawful: 1. Misrepresenting goods or services as those of another; 2. Misrepresenting the source, sponsorship, approval, or certification of goods or services; 3. Misrepresenting the affiliation, connection, or association of the supplier, or of the goods or services, with another; 4. Misrepresenting geographic origin in connection with goods or services; 5. Misrepresenting that goods or services have certain quantities, characteristics, ingredients, uses, or benefits; 6. Misrepresenting that goods or services are of a particular standard, quality, grade, style, or model; 7. Advertising or offering for sale goods that are used, secondhand, repossessed, defective, blemished, deteriorated, or reconditioned, or that are "seconds," irregulars, imperfects, or "not first class," without clearly and unequivocally indicating in the advertisement or offer for sale that the goods are used, secondhand, repossessed, defective, blemished, deteriorated, reconditioned,
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies Virginia law and jurisdiction. The changed text removes the explicit reference to Virginia law and gives InterTrust the power to choose the jurisdiction. This contradicts the Virginia Consumer Protection Act, as the Law Explanation states it could limit the consumer's ability to bring suit in Virginia. Scraped Law Snippet 1 details practices that are considered unlawful under the VCPA, implying that restricting a consumer's legal recourse within Virginia could be interpreted as circumventing these protections. This aligns with the Omission Legal Contradiction definition, because the omission of Virginia law removes a legally mandated consumer protection.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees that if he or she breaches or threatens breach of any of such sections, InterTrust will have available, in addition to any other right or remedy available, the right to obtain an injunction against him or her, from a court of competent jurisdiction restraining such breach or threatened
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees that if he or she breaches or threatens breach of any of such sections, InterTrust will have available the right to obtain an injunction against him or her, restraining such breach or threatened
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'from a court of competent jurisdiction' implies that InterTrust could seek an injunction from any court, even one without proper jurisdiction. This is legally problematic.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 65 outlines the process and requirements for obtaining an injunction in federal court, including the need for a court of competent jurisdiction. Removing this phrase suggests InterTrust could bypass these requirements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The original text specifies seeking an injunction 'from a court of competent jurisdiction,' while the changed text omits this crucial qualification. This omission directly contradicts the principles underlying FRCP 65, which governs injunctions and restraining orders in federal courts. Although Snippet 1 focuses on notice and timing, the Law Explanation highlights that FRCP 65 implies the necessity of a court having proper jurisdiction. By removing the phrase 'from a court of competent jurisdiction,' the modified text suggests that InterTrust could potentially seek an injunction from any court, even one lacking the legal authority to grant such relief, thus creating a legal contradiction. This is not a mere ambiguity; it fundamentally alters the process for obtaining an injunction.

perturbation.original_text: Under no circumstances will any information subject to the export or import laws of any jurisdiction be transferred pursuant to their agreement without proper prior certification and notification of appropriate regulatory offices in applicable jurisdictions and InterTrust.
perturbation.changed_text: Under no circumstances will any information be transferred pursuant to their agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the section about export/import laws, certification, and notification creates a potential violation if the agreement involves the transfer of data subject to export control regulations. It removes the acknowledgment of the legal requirement to comply with such laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Export Administration Regulations (EAR) govern the export and re-export of certain goods, software, and technology. By removing the clause about export/import laws, the agreement fails to acknowledge and ensure compliance with these regulations, potentially leading to violations if controlled information is transferred.

perturbation.justification: The modified text omits the requirement for prior certification and notification related to export/import laws, which is a legal contradiction. The original text acknowledges the necessity of complying with export/import laws, whereas the changed text removes any such acknowledgment. The 'Perturbation Explanation' and 'Law Explanation' both highlight the potential violation of regulations like the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) if controlled information is transferred without proper compliance measures. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions 'Subchapter C Export Administration Regulations' under Title 15, Chapter VII, indicating the existence of regulations governing export-related activities, thus supporting the claim that removing the clause creates a contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 2 refers to the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) and the Commerce Control List (CCL), reinforcing that the export of certain items is regulated. By removing the clause, the contract now does not mention anything about abiding by any export laws.

perturbation.original_text: The parties consent to the personal and exclusive jurisdiction and venue of such court and waive any argument that jurisdiction or venue in such court is improper or inconvenient.
perturbation.changed_text: The parties consent to the jurisdiction and waive any argument that jurisdiction in such court is inconvenient.
perturbation.explanation: Removing 'personal' and 'exclusive', and especially removing 'improper' from the waiver weakens the clause, particularly for the Recipient. A party can only properly waive personal jurisdiction if they have sufficient minimum contacts with the state or district to justify being sued there. Removing 'improper' could imply a waiver even if minimum contacts are lacking.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 12(h)(1) dictates how defenses of lack of personal jurisdiction, improper venue, insufficient process, or insufficient service of process are waived. By removing 'personal', 'exclusive' and changing the explicit waiver of 'improper', the altered clause may not effect a valid waiver of these defenses, potentially creating confusion and legal challenges regarding jurisdiction.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 12. Defenses and Objections: When and How Presented; Motion for Judgment on the Pleadings; Consolidating Motions; Waiving Defenses; Pretrial Hearing (a) Time to Serve a Responsive Pleading. Unless another time is specified by a federal statute, the time for serving a responsive pleading is as follows: (1) In General. (A) A defendant must serve an answer: (i) within 21 days after being served with the summons and complaint; or (ii) if it has timely waived service under Rule 4(d) , within 60 days after the request for a waiver was sent, or within 90 days after it was sent to the defendant outside any judicial district of the United States. (B) A party must serve an answer to a counterclaim or crossclaim within 21 days after being served with the pleading that states the counterclaim or crossclaim. (C) A party must serve a reply to an answer within 21 days after being served with an order to reply, unless the order specifies a different time. (2) United States and Its Agencies, Officers, or Employees Sued in an Official Capacity. The United States, a United States agency, or a United States officer or employee sued only in an official capacity must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the United States attorney. (3) United States Officers or Employees Sued in an Individual Capacity. A United States officer or employee sued in an individual capacity for an act or omission occurring in connection with duties performed on the United States’ behalf must serve an answer to a complaint, counterclaim, or crossclaim within 60 days after service on the officer or employee or service on the United States attorney, whichever is later. perturbation.justification: The omission of key terms like 'personal,' 'exclusive,' and 'improper' in the modified text weakens the waiver of jurisdictional defenses, potentially conflicting with established legal standards for such waivers. While the provided snippets do not directly define the requirements for a valid jurisdictional waiver, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation both suggest that this weakening could undermine the effectiveness of the waiver. The removal of 'improper' especially implies a waiver even when minimum contacts are lacking, increasing the likelihood of the waiver being deemed invalid, therefore a legal contradiction exists.
